indentation-and-blocks.label: Indentation and Blocks
indentation-and-blocks.summary: Understand why indentation controls structure in Python.

multi_choice_1.title: Identifying Indentation Errors
multi_choice_1.prompt: Which of the following code snippets will raise an IndentationError?
multi_choice_1.hint: Check the indentation levels in the snippets.

help.concept: Indentation in Python defines the blocks of code. Incorrect indentation can lead to errors.
help.hint_1: Look for lines that are not properly indented under their respective conditions.
help.hint_2: Remember that all lines in a block must be indented the same amount.

options.a:
if x > 10:
print('x is greater than 10')

fill_blank_choice_1.title: Complete the Indentation
fill_blank_choice_1.prompt:
Fill in the blank to correctly complete the if statement:

```python
if x > 5:
    _____
```
fill_blank_choice_1.hint: Focus on the missing concept or keyword.

help.concept: This task checks the key idea behind complete the indentation.
help.hint_1: Look for the word or phrase that best matches the lesson concept.
help.hint_2: Compare the blank with the meaning of each choice, not just the wording.

choices: print('x is less than or equal to 5')

code_input_1.title: Indentation Practice
code_input_1.prompt: Write a function that checks if a number is positive, negative, or zero. Use proper indentation. Then read the input value, call the function, and print the returned result.
code_input_1.hint: Break the task into the main code steps first.

help.concept: This task checks whether you can apply indentation practice in a working solution.
help.hint_1: Identify the inputs, required steps, and expected result before writing the full solution.
help.hint_2: Build the solution one piece at a time, then check that its behavior matches the prompt.

code_input_2.title: Nested Conditions
code_input_2.prompt: Create a function that checks if a number is even or odd and greater than 10. Use proper indentation. Then read the input value, call the function, and print the returned result.
code_input_2.hint: Break the task into the main code steps first.

help.concept: This task checks whether you can apply nested conditions in a working solution.
help.hint_1: Identify the inputs, required steps, and expected result before writing the full solution.
help.hint_2: Build the solution one piece at a time, then check that its behavior matches the prompt.

code_input_3.title: Multiple Conditions
code_input_3.prompt: Write a function that checks if a number is positive, negative, or zero, and also checks if it's even or odd. Then read the input value, call the function, and print the returned result.
code_input_3.hint: Break the task into the main code steps first.

help.concept: This task checks whether you can apply multiple conditions in a working solution.
help.hint_1: Identify the inputs, required steps, and expected result before writing the full solution.
help.hint_2: Build the solution one piece at a time, then check that its behavior matches the prompt.

indentation-basics.title: Understanding Indentation
indentation-basics.bodyMarkdown:
In Python, indentation is not just for readability; it defines the structure of your code. For example:

```python
if condition:
    # This block is executed if the condition is True
    print('Condition is True')
else:
    # This block is executed if the condition is False
    print('Condition is False')
```

In this example, the indented lines under `if` and `else` are part of their respective blocks. If you forget to indent, Python will raise an `IndentationError`. 

**Your turn:** Try writing your own `if` statement with an `else` block!

Line by line: read each statement from top to bottom. Notice which names are created, which values are passed into functions, and what output the code is meant to show.

nested-indentation.title: Nested Indentation
